summary: Gruber uses App Store and Play Store rankings as a proxy to gauge the momentum of social networks, particularly Twitter/X versus Threads. He notes that Threads ranked #4 in Apple's 2023 top free apps while X didn't make the list at all, and on daily charts X languishes around #63-67 on both stores. He argues these rankings strongly suggest Twitter/X is dying, with Threads helping to kill it. He also delivers a frank assessment of Mastodon's prospects, calling it a successful niche for nerds but one with no mainstream relevance, noting no Mastodon clients crack the top 200 on either store.
key_points: Twitter/X is absent from Apple's 2023 top 20 free apps and sits around #63-67 on daily charts, deep in the long tail of power-curve distributions
Threads ranked #4 for 2023 and #2 on the daily chart (boosted by EU launch), suggesting it's actively siphoning momentum from X
Mastodon has no clients in the top 200 on either app store, suggesting it has peaked as a niche platform with no mainstream trajectory
Truth Social's presence on Play Store (#48) but absence from the App Store top 200 hints at a socio-economic demographic split between Android and iOS users
App store popularity follows power curves, meaning the gap between top-10 apps and those ranked #60+ represents an enormous difference in actual usage
money_quotes: These app stores rankings suggest strongly that Twitter/X is dying, and I think Threads in particular is helping to kill it.
Momentum and traction are hard to gain and easy to lose.
Keep in mind that popularity charts like this follow power curves — #63 is in the long tail.
I enjoy Mastodon, and love Ivory, but Mastodon has seemingly peaked in popularity. It's a niche for nerds.
Regular people do not want to use social networks without algorithmic feeds, and do not want to use social networks whose basic premise they do not understand.
X is down at #67, right below the renowned 'Domino's Pizza USA' app.
key_insight: App store rankings reveal that Twitter/X has lost mainstream momentum catastrophically, Threads is the primary beneficiary, and Mastodon — despite its appeal to technologists — has no path to mainstream relevance.
